content.text: Purpose-built vector stores are designed from the ground up around vector similarity search. Unlike pgvector (which bolts ANN onto PostgreSQL) or Redis Stack (which adds it to a cache), these systems treat the HNSW index as the primary data structure and optimise every layer — storage layout, query planning, payload filtering, and distributed replication — around it.
This sub-subtopic covers the four most widely adopted options in production RAG systems:
. It explains their data models, Python client patterns, metadata filtering, and the operational factors that differentiate them.

attrs.alt: Vector store architecture: ingestion pipeline → collections/HNSW/payload → query → results → LLM

content.text: Despite different APIs, all four share the same logical model:

Chroma is the quickest path from zero to a working vector store. It runs in-process (Python library) or as a standalone server, uses DuckDB + Parquet on disk, and supports a synchronous Python API with no cluster to manage. It is production-capable for read-heavy workloads up to tens of millions of vectors.

import chromadb
from chromadb.config import Settings

# Persistent local store
client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_data")

# Get or create a collection (cosine similarity)
collection = client.get_or_create_collection(
    name="chunks",
    metadata={"hnsw:space": "cosine"},
)

def upsert_chunks(
    ids: list[str],
    embeddings: list[list[float]],
    documents: list[str],
    metadatas: list[dict],
) -> None:
    collection.upsert(
        ids=ids,
        embeddings=embeddings,
        documents=documents,
        metadatas=metadatas,
    )

# Example: upsert a batch
upsert_chunks(
    ids=["chunk-1", "chunk-2"],
    embeddings=[[0.1, 0.2, ...], [0.9, 0.1, ...]],
    documents=["First chunk text", "Second chunk text"],
    metadatas=[
        {"tenant_id": "t1", "lang": "en", "source": "guide.pdf"},
        {"tenant_id": "t1", "lang": "en", "source": "faq.md"},
    ],
)
. Filters are evaluated after ANN retrieval (post-filter), which means a tight filter on a small collection may return fewer than
Qdrant is a Rust-based vector database with first-class support for
(applied during ANN traversal, not after), scalar quantisation to compress RAM usage, and horizontal sharding. It has both a managed cloud offering and a Docker-based self-hosted path.

def search_qdrant(
    query_embedding: list[float],
    tenant_id: str,
    lang: str = "en",
    top_k: int = 10,
    score_threshold: float = 0.75,
) -> list[dict]:
    results = client.search(
        collection_name=COLLECTION,
        query_vector=query_embedding,
        query_filter=Filter(
            must=[
                FieldCondition(key="tenant_id", match=MatchValue(value=tenant_id)),
                FieldCondition(key="lang",      match=MatchValue(value=lang)),
            ]
        ),
        limit=top_k,
        score_threshold=score_threshold,
        with_payload=True,
    )
    return [
        {"content": r.payload.get("content", ""), "score": r.score}
        for r in results
    ]
payload filters in Qdrant are applied
the HNSW graph traversal when a filterable index exists on the payload field. Create it with
so Qdrant can use a filtered HNSW graph instead of post-filtering.
Weaviate uses a GraphQL API and defines schemas as typed
with strongly typed properties. It includes a built-in module system for automatic vectorisation (you can supply embeddings yourself or configure a
module to call an embedding provider). Its hybrid search natively combines BM25 and vector search via the

import weaviate

client = weaviate.Client("http://localhost:8080")

# Define schema class (run once)
schema = {
    "class": "Chunk",
    "vectorizer": "none",  # we supply embeddings ourselves
    "properties": [
        {"name": "content",   "dataType": ["text"]},
        {"name": "tenant_id", "dataType": ["text"]},
        {"name": "doc_id",    "dataType": ["text"]},
        {"name": "chunk_idx", "dataType": ["int"]},
        {"name": "lang",      "dataType": ["text"]},
    ],
}
if not client.schema.exists("Chunk"):
    client.schema.create_class(schema)

def upsert_chunk_weaviate(
    uuid: str, content: str, embedding: list[float], tenant_id: str, doc_id: str, chunk_idx: int, lang: str = "en"
) -> None:
    client.data_object.create(
        data_object={
            "content":   content,
            "tenant_id": tenant_id,
            "doc_id":    doc_id,
            "chunk_idx": chunk_idx,
            "lang":      lang,
        },
        class_name="Chunk",
        uuid=uuid,
        vector=embedding,
    )

def search_weaviate(
    query_text: str,
    query_embedding: list[float],
    tenant_id: str,
    top_k: int = 10,
) -> list[dict]:
    result = (
        client.query
        .get("Chunk", ["content", "doc_id"])
        .with_hybrid(query=query_text, vector=query_embedding, alpha=0.5)
        .with_where({
            "path": ["tenant_id"],
            "operator": "Equal",
            "valueText": tenant_id,
        })
        .with_limit(top_k)
        .with_additional(["score", "explainScore"])
        .do()
    )
    return result["data"]["Get"]["Chunk"]
parameter blends BM25 (alpha=0) and pure vector (alpha=1) search.
gives equal weight to both; tune it toward 0 for exact-match-heavy queries and toward 1 for semantic queries.

Milvus is designed for very large scale: billions of vectors across distributed nodes. It separates storage (object store like S3 or MinIO) from computation, and scales query and index nodes independently. It also supports multiple vector fields per collection, which is useful for multi-modal applications (text + image embeddings in one schema).

from pymilvus import connections, FieldSchema, CollectionSchema, DataType, Collection, utility

connections.connect(host="localhost", port="19530")

fields = [
    FieldSchema(name="id",        dtype=DataType.INT64,         is_primary=True, auto_id=True),
    FieldSchema(name="tenant_id", dtype=DataType.VARCHAR,       max_length=64),
    FieldSchema(name="content",   dtype=DataType.VARCHAR,       max_length=4096),
    FieldSchema(name="embedding", dtype=DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR,  dim=1536),
]
schema = CollectionSchema(fields, description="RAG chunks")

if not utility.has_collection("chunks"):
    col = Collection("chunks", schema)
    col.create_index(
        field_name="embedding",
        index_params={"index_type": "HNSW", "metric_type": "COSINE", "params": {"M": 16, "efConstruction": 200}},
    )
else:
    col = Collection("chunks")
col.load()
All four stores share the same logical model: collections, embedded vectors, filterable payload, and ANN top-k queries.
is ideal for prototypes and small-to-medium production workloads; zero infrastructure overhead.
excels when filter selectivity is high: its pre-filter HNSW keeps recall high even with narrow where-clauses.
is the strongest choice when you need built-in BM25 + vector hybrid search and a rich module ecosystem.
is the choice for billion-scale deployments requiring independent scaling of ingest and query nodes.
For most single-tenant or small-multi-tenant RAG applications, Chroma or Qdrant provide the best developer experience with acceptable production characteristics.
